docs.title: Mobile Hoverboard Robot Base
docs.text:
The Mobile Hoverboard Robot Base is a low-cost, modular mobile robot base developed by the Cornell Tech Interaction Lab (CT-IRL). Built from a deconstructed hoverboard, it is designed to be attached to everyday objects — like a trash bin, a cart, or furniture — to create novel robots for Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) research. Operators control it remotely using the Wizard of Oz method, simulating autonomous behavior.

Platform in action — trash bin configuration

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The platform is built around a deconstructed hoverboard driven by an ODrive motor controller, managed by a Raspberry Pi running ROS 2 Humble. A second Raspberry Pi with an 8BitDo Lite 2 Bluetooth controller provides wireless teleoperation.

Key capabilities:

  • Differential drive locomotion via hoverboard motors
  • Mounted Insta360 for first-person video
  • Wizard of Oz operation for HRI studies

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text: Section Description Bill of Materials Parts list with sourcing links Assembly: Frame Dolly and mounting plate setup Assembly: Electronics ODrive, RPi, and wiring Software: Installation Ubuntu + ROS 2 setup on Raspberry Pi Software: Calibration ODrive motor calibration Teleoperation Bluetooth joystick control

docs.title: Two-RPi Architecture
docs.text: Each robot unit uses two Raspberry Pis — one mounted inside the base (runs the motor controller) and one carried externally by the operator (runs the joystick controller). They communicate over a shared WiFi network via ROS 2.

docs.title: Related Repositories
docs.text:
• Summer25_Trashbot
  • Mobile_HRI_Lab_Hub — course lab materials
  • mobilehri2023 — ROS 2 packages for joystick mapping and motor control

docs.title: Bill of Materials
docs.text: All parts needed to build one Trashbot unit.

docs.title: Chassis & Frame
docs.text: Qty Part Source 1 Deconstructed Hoverboard Walmart | eBay 1 Trash Can Dolly with Wheels (Rubbermaid BRUTE) Amazon 1 Trash Bin Home Depot

docs.title: Drive System
docs.text: Qty Part Source 1 ODrive Motor Controller v3.6 ODrive Robotics 1 XT60 Power Cable Amazon 1 Lithium-Ion Battery (if hoverboard battery not usable) Amazon

docs.title: Filtering PCB (per motor)
docs.text:
ODrive v3.6 has some issues with hall sensors Forum link. Solution involves using 22 nF capacitors to filter out the noise. Newer ODrives may not encounter this issue, but we have not tested them yet.

Qty Part Source 6 22 nF Capacitors DigiKey

docs.title: Compute & Control
docs.text: Qty Part Source 2 Raspberry Pi 4 (or newer) RPi4 2 Power Banks Amazon 1 8BitDo Lite 2 Bluetooth Controller Amazon

docs.title: Laser Cut Files (for trash barrel robot example)
docs.text:
Download the templates from the images directory:

  • trashbot_guidlines_all.ai — full drilling template
  • trashbot_guidlines_inner.ai — inner circular mounting plate

docs.title: Research
docs.text: The CT-IRL Mobile Platform has been used across multiple published studies on Human-Robot Interaction in public spaces. Below is a summary of the research conducted using this platform, led by Fanjun (Frank) Bu and the Cornell Tech Interaction Lab.

docs.title: Trash Barrel Robots in the Wild
docs.text:
The platform is best known for the Trash Barrel Robot deployments — hoverboard-based mobile bases with trash barrels attached, deployed in real public spaces in New York City using the Wizard of Oz method.

A trash barrel robot follows a janitor through Manhattan streets. Photo: Cornell Chronicle

Coverage by IEEE Spectrum: "Humans (Mostly) Love Trash Robots"

docs.title: Trash Barrel Robot Studies
docs.text:
Making Sense of Robots in Public Spaces: A Study of Trash Barrel Robots

THRI 2025 · Fanjun Bu, Kerstin Fischer, Wendy Ju

Paper ↗

Trash in Motion: Emergent Interactions with Robotic Trashcans in a Public Square

CHI 2024 · Barry Brown, Fanjun Bu, Ilan Mandel, Wendy Ju

Paper ↗

Trash Barrel Robots in the City

HRI 2023 · Fanjun Bu*, Ilan Mandel*, Wen-Ying Lee, Wendy Ju

Paper ↗

docs.title: Wizard of Oz & Field Deployment
docs.text:
The People Behind the Robots: How Wizards Wrangle Robots in Public Deployments

CHI 2025 · Hannah R. M. Pelikan, Fanjun Bu, Wendy Ju

Paper ↗

Field Notes on Deploying Research Robots in Public Spaces

CHI Extended Abstracts 2024 · Fanjun Bu, Alexandra Bremers, Mark Colley, Wendy Ju

Paper ↗

docs.title: Social Intelligence & Navigation
docs.text:
Using Vision-Language Models as Proxies for Social Intelligence in Human-Robot Interaction

arXiv 2025 · Fanjun Bu, Melina Tsai, Audrey Tjokro, Tapomayukh Bhattacharjee, Jorge Ortiz, Wendy Ju

Examines how robots can interpret nonverbal cues (gaze, personal space) to decide when engaging with people is appropriate, combining lightweight detectors with vision-language model queries.

Paper ↗

The Robotability Score: Enabling Harmonious Robot Navigation on Urban Streets

CHI 2025 · Matt Franchi*, Maria Teresa Parreira*, Fanjun Bu*, Wendy Ju

Paper ↗

SSUP-HRI: Social Signaling in Urban Public Human-Robot Interaction Dataset

ICSR 2024 · Fanjun Bu, Wendy Ju

Paper ↗

ReStory: VLM-Augmentation of Social Human-Robot Interaction Datasets

ICSR 2024 · Fanjun Bu, Wendy Ju

Paper ↗

docs.title: Platform Configurations
docs.text:
The same hoverboard base has been deployed in multiple configurations:

Trash BarrelChair

A standard trash barrel mounted on the mobile base. Deployed in Manhattan plazas as part of HRI'23 and CHI'24 studies.

An office chair mounted on the base, demonstrating the platform's modularity for attaching to everyday furniture.

docs.title: Press Coverage
docs.text: Outlet Date Title CNN Business April 12, 2023 These robotic trash cans were filmed to test human-robotic interactions NPR April 16, 2023 Researchers released robot trash cans in NYC to see how people would react Cornell Chronicle April 19, 2023 (Almost) everyone likes a helpful trash robot USA TODAY April 15, 2023 Robo-trash cans patrolling New York plaza make friends, creep out some IEEE Spectrum March 10, 2023 Humans (Mostly) Love Trash Robots

docs.title: Teleoperation
docs.text:
The Mobile Hoverboard Robot Base is teleoperated using an 8BitDo Lite 2 Bluetooth gamepad connected to the external Raspberry Pi. This enables the Wizard of Oz operation style used in HRI experiments.

8BitDo Lite 2 Bluetooth Controller

docs.title: Part A: Connect the Controller via Bluetooth
docs.text:
1. SSH into the controller RPi
  2. Install Bluetooth packages if not already present: 
    sudo apt install -y bluez blueman

  3. Open the Bluetooth manager (via VNC Viewer for GUI, or use bluetoothctl):

    bluetoothctl (CLI)Blueman (GUI via VNC) 

    bluetoothctl
    Then inside bluetoothctl: 
    power on
agent on
scan on
    Boot the 8BitDo into wireless pairing mode (hold the pairing button). When it appears in the scan list, pair and connect: 
    pair <MAC_ADDRESS>
connect <MAC_ADDRESS>
trust <MAC_ADDRESS>

    Open Blueman from the desktop, search for the controller, and connect.

  4. The controller LED turns blue when connected.

docs.title: Connect by MAC Address
docs.text:
Once you've paired the controller once, you can reconnect directly:

bluetoothctl
connect <MAC_ADDRESS>

The MAC address of your 8BitDo controller can be found during the scan on step — it will appear as 8BitDo Lite 2 in the device list.

docs.title: Part B: Read Joystick Messages
docs.text:
Verify the controller is publishing to ROS 2:

ros2 run joy joy_node
ros2 topic echo /joy

Expected output: 

header:
  frame_id: joy
axes:
- -0.0   # Left stick X
- -0.0   # Left stick Y
- 1.0    # Left trigger
- -0.0   # Right stick X
- -0.0   # Right stick Y
- 1.0    # Right trigger
- 0.0    # D-pad X
- 0.0    # D-pad Y
buttons:
- 0
- 0
- 0
...

All axes are continuous floats (−1.0 to 1.0). All buttons are binary integers (0 or 1).

docs.title: Part C: Haptic Feedback (Rumble)
docs.text:
Test that the rumble motor works — useful for signaling states during experiments:

ros2 topic pub -r 10 /joy/set_feedback sensor_msgs/msg/JoyFeedback \
  "{type: 1, id: 0, intensity: 0.9}"

JoyFeedback message types:

Value Type 0 LED 1 Rumble 2 Buzzer

docs.title: Part D: Button Mapping
docs.text:
The key mapping node (keymapping_node.py) translates joystick axes to /cmd_vel (linear + angular velocity).

Safety button

L1 must be held to enable driving. This prevents unintentional movement.

Launch the mapping:

cd ~/mobilehri_ws
colcon build && source install/setup.bash
ros2 launch joy_teleop_keymapping mapping_launch.py

Monitor the output: 

ros2 topic echo /cmd_vel

Feel free to customize the button mapping in keymapping_node.py — the 8BitDo has many buttons, triggers, and axes to work with.

docs.title: Part E: Drive with the Hoverboard
docs.text:
Once both the mapping node and the mobile base controller are running (see Running the Robot):

ros2 launch mobile_robot_control mobile_robot_launch.py

Hold L1 and use the left stick to drive. Place the trash bin on top and start your HRI experiment!

docs.title: Safety
docs.text:
Warning

  • Always hold L1 as a deadman switch — releasing it stops the robot.
  • Do not leave the hoverboard battery plugged into the ODrive when the robot is unattended.
  • The hoverboard chassis is conductive — keep it clear of loose electronics.

docs.title: Assembly Overview
docs.text:
This section walks through the complete assembly of Trashbot.

Before you start

  • Review the Bill of Materials and confirm you have all parts.
  • Download the laser cut files from the images directory before your first session.
  • Tools needed: screwdrivers, drill, soldering iron, wire strippers.

docs.title: Assembly Steps
docs.text:
Follow the guides in order:

  1. Frame — Set up the dolly and attach the laser-cut circular mounting plate
  2. Electronics & Wiring — Mount ODrive, build filtering PCBs, wire motors and Raspberry Pi
  3. Drive System — Understand the hoverboard differential drive system
  4. Sensors & Accessories — Mount GoPro and attach the trash bin

docs.title: Tools Required
docs.text:
• Phillips and flathead screwdrivers
  • Power drill (for dolly holes)
  • Soldering iron and solder
  • Wire strippers
  • Multimeter
  • Laptop (for SSH and ODrive tool)
  • Access to a laser cutter

docs.title: Laser Cut Files
docs.text:
Both files are in the images directory:

File Purpose trashbot_guidlines_all.ai Full drilling template for the dolly trashbot_guidlines_inner.ai Circular mounting plate

docs.title: Drive System
docs.text: The Trashbot uses the motors and wheels from a deconstructed hoverboard, controlled by an ODrive v3.6 motor controller. The hoverboard provides two brushless DC hub motors with built-in hall sensors.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
The platform uses differential drive kinematics. The two hoverboard wheel motors are driven independently — varying their relative speeds produces turning.

Differential drive kinematics

The kinematic equations used in the software:

\[v_l = \frac{v - \frac{l \cdot \omega}{2}}{r}\] \[v_r = \frac{v + \frac{l \cdot \omega}{2}}{r}\]

Where:

  • \(v\) = commanded linear velocity (m/s)
  • \(\omega\) = commanded angular velocity (rad/s)
  • \(l\) = wheel separation (m)
  • \(r\) = wheel radius (m)
  • \(v_l, v_r\) = left and right wheel velocities (turns/s sent to ODrive)

docs.title: ODrive Motor Controller
docs.text:
The ODrive v3.6 is a high-performance open-source motor controller. It connects to the Raspberry Pi via USB and accepts velocity commands in turns/second.

  • Product page: ODrive v3.6
  • ODrive hoverboard guide: docs.odriverobotics.com

See Calibration for ODrive setup and tuning.

Hoverboard-specific tuning

ODrive parameters (current limits, velocity limits, pole pairs) vary by hoverboard model. The config file in the repo is tuned for the specific boards in the lab. If you use a different hoverboard, you may need to adjust these values.

docs.title: Electronics & Wiring
docs.text:
This guide covers mounting the ODrive and all components onto the robot frame after ODrive Calibration is complete.

Safety

Unplug the battery before touching any electronics. The hoverboard metal chassis is conductive — never place electronics directly on it. Cover all dangling wires with electrical tape.

docs.title: Step 1: Mount ODrive to Circular Plate
docs.text:
Secure the ODrive to the circular mounting plate using screws.

ODrive motor controller

ODrive secured to circular mounting plate

docs.title: Step 2: Connect Both Wheels
docs.text:
With both wheels calibrated, connect all wiring to the mounted ODrive:

  • Screw the phase wires (U, V, W) from each hub motor into the ODrive terminal blocks — M0 for the left wheel, M1 for the right wheel
  • Plug the hall sensor JST connectors into the filtering PCBs on each axis port

docs.title: Step 3: Connect ODrive to Raspberry Pi
docs.text: Connect the micro-USB cable from the ODrive to the base Raspberry Pi.

docs.title: Step 4: Mount the Circular Plate
docs.text: Mount the fully wired circular plate assembly onto the dolly frame.

docs.title: Step 5: Plug In Power
docs.text:
Connect the hoverboard battery. The ODrive power LED should light up.

Fully assembled and wired robot front

docs.title: Frame Assembly
docs.text: This guide covers setting up the dolly base and attaching the laser-cut circular mounting plate.

docs.title: Parts Required
docs.text: Qty Part 1 Trash Can Dolly with Wheels 1 Circular Mounting Plate (laser cut) ~10 Screws ~8 Nuts 2 Screwdrivers

docs.title: Laser Cut Files
docs.text:
Download the templates before starting:

  • trashbot_guidlines_all.ai — full drilling template
  • trashbot_guidlines_inner.ai — inner circular mounting plate

Laser cut template

docs.title: Part A: Setting Up the Dolly
Step 1: Remove the circular top of the dolly
docs.text: Original dolly chassis

docs.title: Step 2: Laser-cut the drilling template and the circular plate
docs.text: Cut both the drilling template and the mounting plate using the .ai files above.

docs.title: Step 3: Align and drill outer holes
docs.text:
Place the drilling template over the dolly and align it. Mark the 4 pre-existing screw hole locations.

Drilling the outer mounting holes

docs.title: Step 4: Secure the dolly
docs.text: Drill 4 matching holes and secure the dolly using 4 screws and 4 nuts.

docs.title: Part B: Attaching the Circular Mounting Plate
Step 5: Align the circular plate
docs.text: Align the laser-cut circular plate with the 4 inner mounting holes on the dolly.

docs.title: Step 6: Attach with screws and nuts
docs.text:
Secure using 4 screws and 4 nuts through the inner mounting holes.

Inner circular mounting plate

docs.title: Sensors & Accessories
docs.text: This page covers the sensors and accessories mounted on the Trashbot.

docs.title: Insta360 Camera
docs.text:
The Trashbot mounts a GoPro for first-person video during HRI experiments.

GoPro — front view of mount

GoPro — back view of mount

docs.title: WiFi
docs.text: A WiFi router provides the network connection between the two Raspberry Pis (base and controller) and any external laptop.

docs.title: Trash Bin
docs.text:
The trash bin is placed on top of the platform after all hardware and wiring is complete.

Tip

Place the trash bin on top only after completing and testing all electronics. The bin can be lifted off quickly for debugging.

docs.title: Software Overview
docs.text: The Mobile Hoverboard Robot Base runs ROS 2 Humble on Ubuntu 22.04 across two Raspberry Pis.

docs.title: Two-RPi Architecture
docs.text:
RPi Location Role Base RPi Inside the robot Runs ODrive motor controller (mobile_robot_control) Controller RPi External, carried by operator Runs joystick node and key mapping (joy_teleop_keymapping)

The two RPis communicate over ROS 2 on a shared WiFi network. The operator uses the 8BitDo controller → controller RPi → /cmd_vel topic → base RPi → ODrive → motors.

docs.title: Software Stack
docs.text: Component Package OS Ubuntu 22.04 LTS Middleware ROS 2 Humble Joystick input ros-humble-joy Button mapping joy_teleop_keymapping Motor control mobile_robot_control Motor driver ODrive v3.6 via odrivetool / USB

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
Follow these guides in order:

  1. Installation — Ubuntu + ROS 2 + workspace setup
  2. Calibration — ODrive motor calibration
  3. Running the Robot — Full startup sequence

docs.title: Source Repository
docs.text: https://github.com/FAR-Lab/mobilehri2023

docs.title: ODrive Calibration
docs.text:
Calibrate each motor individually on the bench before assembling the robot. This sets the motor parameters and verifies the hall sensor connections. Do this once per robot (or after a config reset).

Warning

The motor will beep and spin during calibration. Clamp the wheel securely — never hold it by hand.

After Calibration

Once both wheels are calibrated, proceed to Assembly to mount everything onto the robot frame.

docs.title: Part A: Bench Hardware Setup
docs.text: Before running any software, set up the ODrive and one wheel on the bench.

docs.title: A1. Clamp the Wheel
docs.text: Remove one wheel from the hoverboard with a hex key. Clamp the axle firmly to a desk — the wheel must be free to spin but the axle must not move.

docs.title: A2. Build the Filtering PCBs
docs.text:
Solder three 22 nF capacitors onto each breadboard (one per motor). These filter noise on the hall sensor lines. See the ODrive hall state post for details.

Filtering PCB — step 1

Filtering PCB — step 2

Complete breadboard wiring reference

Filtering PCB pinout — five pins from GND to 5V

docs.title: A3. Insert Hoverboard Wires to ODrive
docs.text:
Insert hoverboard wires (yellow, blue, green) from the motor to the ODrive board. You may need to solder wire extensions if the wires are too short. Cover any dangling white wires with electrical tape.

The five PCB pins go from GND to 5V — the red wire connects to the 5V pin labeled on the ODrive.

Yellow / Blue / Green hall sensor connections

docs.title: A4. Insert Brake Resistor into ODrive
docs.text:
Insert the brake resistor into the designated slot in the middle of the ODrive board.

Brake resistor placement on ODrive

docs.title: A5. Connect XT60 Power Cable
docs.text:
Connect the XT60 power cable from the hoverboard battery to the ODrive power input.

Polarity

Red wire = positive, black wire = negative. Reversing polarity will damage the ODrive.

docs.title: A6. Connect ODrive to Raspberry Pi
docs.text: Connect a micro-USB cable from the ODrive to the base Raspberry Pi.

docs.title: A9. Plug In Power
docs.text:
Connect the hoverboard battery. The ODrive power LED should light up.

Complete bench setup — ODrive connected to one wheel and Raspberry Pi

docs.title: B1. Flash the Raspberry Pi Image
docs.text:
Use the Raspberry Pi Imager to write the class image to an SD card, then boot the base RPi.

Download class RPi image

docs.title: B2. SSH into the Raspberry Pi
docs.text:
ssh ubuntu@<base-rpi-ip>

The IP address is displayed on the miniTFT screen after boot.

docs.title: B3. Download the Lab Config File
docs.text:
curl -LJO https://raw.githubusercontent.com/FAR-Lab/Mobile_HRI_Lab_Hub/main/Lab3/mobilehri_config.json
odrivetool restore-config mobilehri_config.json

Note

Ignore warnings about parameters not being loaded — you will recalibrate the motors anyway. ODrive parameters may vary between hoverboard models; consult the ODrive hoverboard guide if needed.

docs.title: B4. Open ODrive Tool
docs.text:
odrivetool

The terminal should show Connected to ODrive xxxxxxxx and a >>> prompt.

ODrive tool connected

docs.title: B5. Calibrate Axis 0 (First Wheel)
docs.text:
odrv0.clear_errors()
odrv0.axis0.requested_state = AXIS_STATE_FULL_CALIBRATION_SEQUENCE

Wait for the motor to beep and spin briefly (~10–15 seconds), then check for errors:

dump_errors(odrv0)

Expected output — no errors after successful calibration

Check for errors under axis0. If you see an ILLEGAL_HALL_STATE error, check that the filtering PCB is plugged in correctly. See the ODrive Hall State troubleshooting thread for more help.

If no errors, save and reboot:

odrv0.save_configuration()
odrv0.reboot()

docs.title: B6. Test Axis 0
docs.text:
odrv0.axis0.requested_state = AXIS_STATE_CLOSED_LOOP_CONTROL
odrv0.axis0.controller.input_vel = 2   # 2 turns/second

Note the spinning direction. To stop:

odrv0.axis0.controller.input_vel = 0
odrv0.axis0.requested_state = AXIS_STATE_IDLE

docs.title: B7. Calibrate Axis 1 (Second Wheel)
docs.text:
Warning

Unplug the battery before swapping wheels. Remove the first wheel's JST connector and phase wires, then connect the second wheel to axis 1.

Reconnect the second wheel's hall sensor JST to the axis 1 filtering PCB port and screw the phase wires into M1.

Second wheel connected to axis 1

Plug in power and reconnect with odrivetool, then:

odrv0.clear_errors()
odrv0.axis1.requested_state = AXIS_STATE_FULL_CALIBRATION_SEQUENCE
dump_errors(odrv0)

Check errors under axis1. If no errors:

odrv0.save_configuration()
odrv0.reboot()

Test axis 1:

odrv0.axis1.requested_state = AXIS_STATE_CLOSED_LOOP_CONTROL
odrv0.axis1.controller.input_vel = 2
odrv0.axis1.controller.input_vel = 0
odrv0.axis1.requested_state = AXIS_STATE_IDLE

Both wheels calibrated — proceed to Assembly.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
This guide walks through setting up Ubuntu 22.04 and ROS 2 Humble on the Raspberry Pi.

Pre-configured image

The lab maintains a pre-configured Raspberry Pi image with ROS 2, the workspace, and all dependencies already set up. If you have access to it, flash that image and skip to Running the Robot.

Download class RPi image · How to flash with RPi Imager

docs.title: 1. Install Ubuntu 22.04 on Raspberry Pi
docs.text:
Use the Raspberry Pi Imager to write Ubuntu 22.04 LTS (64-bit) to an SD card.

  • Download Ubuntu for RPi: ubuntu.com/download/raspberry-pi

docs.title: 2. Install ROS 2 Humble
docs.text:
Follow the official ROS 2 Humble installation guide, or use the commands below.

# Set locale
sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y locales
sudo locale-gen en_US en_US.UTF-8
sudo update-locale LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8 LANG=en_US.UTF-8
export LANG=en_US.UTF-8

# Add ROS 2 apt repository
sudo apt install -y software-properties-common curl
sudo curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ros/rosdistro/master/ros.key \
  -o /usr/share/keyrings/ros-archive-keyring.gpg
echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/ros-archive-keyring.gpg] \
  http://packages.ros.org/ros2/ubuntu $(. /etc/os-release && echo $UBUNTU_CODENAME) main" \
  | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/ros2.list > /dev/null

# Install ROS 2 Humble desktop
sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y ros-humble-desktop

# Install joy package for joystick support
sudo apt install -y ros-humble-joy

docs.title: 3. Source the ROS 2 Environment
docs.text:
You need to source ROS 2 every time you open a new terminal. To do this automatically:

echo "source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash" >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

docs.title: 4. Set Up the ROS 2 Workspace
docs.text:
mkdir -p ~/mobilehri_ws/src
cd ~/mobilehri_ws/src

# Clone the mobile HRI control packages
git clone https://github.com/FAR-Lab/mobilehri2023.git

cd ~/mobilehri_ws
colcon build
source install/setup.bash

Add the workspace to your .bashrc so it's always sourced:

echo "source ~/mobilehri_ws/install/setup.bash" >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

docs.title: 5. Install ODrive Tool
docs.text:
The ODrive tool is used to configure and calibrate the motor controller.

Follow instructions here.

docs.title: Running the Robot
docs.text: This page covers the full startup sequence for operating the platform. Two Raspberry Pis must be running simultaneously — one inside the robot (base) and one external (controller).

docs.title: Terminal Overview
docs.text:
Running the robot requires 5 terminals across the two Raspberry Pis:

Terminal RPi Command 1 Controller RPi ros2 run joy joy_node 2 Controller RPi ros2 topic echo /joy (debug) 3 Controller RPi ros2 launch joy_teleop_keymapping mapping_launch.py 4 Controller RPi ros2 topic echo /cmd_vel (debug) 5 Base RPi ros2 launch mobile_robot_control mobile_robot_launch.py

docs.title: Step 1: Calibrate the ODrive
docs.text: Before launching, complete ODrive calibration on the base RPi.

docs.title: Step 2: SSH into Both Raspberry Pis
docs.text:
Base RPi (internal — runs motor controller): 

ssh ubuntu@<base-rpi-ip>

Controller RPi (external — runs joystick): 

ssh ubuntu@<controller-rpi-ip>

Replace the IP addresses with those assigned to your Raspberry Pis on your network.

docs.title: Step 3: Launch on the Controller RPi
docs.text:
Terminal 1 — Start the joy node (reads joystick input): 

source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash
ros2 run joy joy_node

Terminal 3 — Launch the key mapping node (maps joystick to /cmd_vel): 

cd ~/mobilehri_ws
colcon build
source install/setup.bash
ros2 launch joy_teleop_keymapping mapping_launch.py

docs.title: Step 4: Launch on the Base RPi
docs.text:
Terminal 5 — Launch the mobile base controller (reads /cmd_vel, drives motors): 

cd ~/mobilehri_ws
colcon build
source install/setup.bash
ros2 launch mobile_robot_control mobile_robot_launch.py

docs.title: Step 5: Verify
docs.text:
Terminal 2 (controller RPi) — confirm joystick data is flowing: 

source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash
ros2 topic echo /joy

Terminal 4 (controller RPi) — confirm velocity commands are being generated: 

ros2 topic echo /cmd_vel

docs.title: Step 6: Drive!
docs.text: Use the 8BitDo Lite 2 controller to drive the robot. Place the attachment (trash bin, chair, etc.) on top before operating in public.

docs.title: Quick Start Scripts
docs.text:
Both robots have convenience scripts that wrap the above:

# On the base RPi (after calibration):
./start_mobile_base.sh

# On the controller RPi (after base is running):
./start_controller.sh

docs.title: Shutdown
docs.text:
Always shut down the Raspberry Pis gracefully before cutting power:

sudo shutdown now

Then disconnect the hoverboard battery.
